articles.label: Infrastructure
articles.headline: Pensacola Beach renourishment project stalls as county disputes Army Corps cost estimates
articles.dek: Escambia County commissioners balked at a $14 million price tag increase last week, leaving the eroded shoreline west of Portofino without a clear path forward before hurricane season.
brief: Escambia County commissioners rejected a revised cost-sharing agreement with the Army Corps of Engineers after estimates jumped from $31 million to $45 million
The 2.3-mile stretch of beach west of Portofino has lost an average of 47 feet of shoreline since Hurricane Sally in 2020
Without action before June 1, the project cannot proceed until after the 2026 hurricane season at earliest
articles.body:
The sand runs out a hundred yards west of the Portofino tower. What remains of the beach narrows to a strip barely wide enough for a beach chair before disappearing entirely into a tangle of sea oats and exposed limestone.

This is where Pensacola Beach ends now. And for at least another year, it may stay that way.

Escambia County commissioners voted 4-1 last Thursday to reject a revised cost-sharing agreement with the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers for the long-delayed Western Reach renourishment project. The reason: a $14 million increase in estimated costs that the county says materialized without adequate explanation.

"We're not writing a blank check," said District 2 Commissioner Mike Kohler, whose district includes Pensacola Beach. "The Corps came to us eighteen months ago with one number. Now they want 45 percent more and can't tell us where the money goes."

The math

The original 2024 agreement pegged total project costs at $31.2 million. Under the standard 65-35 federal-local split, Escambia County's share came to roughly $10.9 million. County budget documents show commissioners approved that figure in October 2024.

The revised estimate, presented at a March 27 workshop, lists total costs at $45.1 million. The county's new share: $15.8 million.

Army Corps Mobile District spokesperson Lt. Col. Jennifer Hartwell said in an email that the increase reflects "updated dredging contract rates, fuel cost adjustments, and revised sediment transport modeling." She declined to provide a line-item breakdown, citing ongoing procurement processes.

Commissioner Jeff Bergosh, who cast the lone vote to accept the revised agreement, called the rejection shortsighted.

"We can argue about the number all we want," Bergosh said. "Meanwhile the beach keeps washing away. Every storm that comes through, we lose more."

Six years of erosion

The Western Reach—the 2.3-mile stretch from Portofino Island Resort to the Escambia-Santa Rosa county line—has never fully recovered from Hurricane Sally in September 2020.

That Category 2 storm parked over Pensacola Beach for nearly 18 hours. The slow-moving system pulled sand offshore and deposited it in nearshore bars that subsequent storms have scattered rather than returned.

According to the Santa Rosa Island Authority's 2025 shoreline survey, the Western Reach has lost an average of 47 feet of beach width since pre-Sally measurements. Some sections show losses exceeding 60 feet.

SRIA Executive Director Paolo Ghio said the erosion has reached a tipping point.

"We're not talking about aesthetics anymore," Ghio said in an interview Tuesday. "We're talking about infrastructure. Via de Luna is closer to the water than it's ever been. Another significant storm surge event and we could be looking at road damage."

Via de Luna Drive, the only road connecting Pensacola Beach's western residential areas to the main commercial district, runs within 200 feet of the mean high water line in several locations along the Western Reach.

The clock

The timing of Thursday's vote creates its own complications.

Army Corps regulations prohibit beach nourishment dredging operations during sea turtle nesting season, which runs May 1 through October 31 in Northwest Florida. The Corps also maintains a policy of avoiding coastal construction projects during the Atlantic hurricane season's peak months.

Those windows leave a narrow period—roughly November through April—when work can proceed.

Lt. Col. Hartwell said the earliest the Corps could mobilize dredging equipment, assuming an agreement is reached, would be late November 2026. But she added that timeline depends on a signed agreement by early May.

"Contracting and mobilization require lead time," she wrote. "A May agreement allows for a November start. Later than that and we're looking at 2027."

Commissioner Kohler said he wants the county to pursue independent cost verification before any new vote.

"We're talking about tax dollars," he said. "We have a responsibility to know what we're buying."

Tourist dollars, tourism questions

The dispute arrives as Pensacola Beach enters its peak tourism season. Visit Pensacola reported 2.87 million overnight visitors to Escambia County beaches in 2025, generating an estimated $892 million in direct spending.

But those numbers aggregate the entire island. The Western Reach, with its limited beach access and eroded shoreline, draws a fraction of the traffic seen at Casino Beach or Park East.

Short-term rental data from AirDNA shows Western Reach properties averaging 52 percent occupancy in 2025, compared to 71 percent for properties near the main public beach. Average nightly rates ran $47 lower.

Sarah Chen, who owns two rental condos at Portofino, said the beach conditions have hurt her bookings.

"Guests look at photos and see the beach," Chen said. "They get here and there's barely any sand. The reviews reflect that. I've had people demand refunds."

The Portofino Owners Association did not respond to requests for comment on whether the condo complex plans any private beach nourishment efforts.

What comes next

County Administrator Wes Moreno said staff will present options at the April 17 commission meeting. Those could include requesting a detailed cost breakdown from the Corps, seeking alternative funding sources or pursuing a smaller-scale interim project.

The Santa Rosa Island Authority has offered to contribute an additional $2 million from its beach maintenance reserves toward the county's share, according to Ghio. That offer is contingent on the full project moving forward.

"Partial measures don't work," Ghio said. "You can't renourish half a reach and expect it to hold. The sand migrates. You either do the whole thing or you're throwing money away."

For now, the beach west of Portofino remains as it was Thursday morning: narrow, scarped and waiting.

articles.label: Military
articles.headline: Blue Angels return to Pensacola for homecoming show amid tighter base access rules
articles.dek: NAS Pensacola's new security protocols will limit gate capacity for the April 25-26 airshow, pushing more spectators to off-base viewing areas and Pensacola Beach.
brief: NAS Pensacola will cap gate entry at 25,000 vehicles for the 2026 homecoming airshow, down from previous years' unrestricted access
New DBIDS credential requirements mean all adult visitors must pre-register online by April 20 or face turned away at gates
Pensacola Beach and Casino Beach parking lots will serve as primary overflow viewing areas with shuttle service to Quietwater
articles.body:
The line of cars stretched back past the Navy Federal Credit Union headquarters on Navy Boulevard at 6:14 a.m. on a Saturday last April. By 8 a.m., when the gates officially opened, it reached the I-110 overpass. By noon, base police had closed two of three entry points entirely.

That won't happen this year. NAS Pensacola has announced vehicle caps and mandatory pre-registration requirements for the 2026 Blue Angels Homecoming Air Show on April 25-26, fundamentally changing how the region's signature military event operates.

"We heard the feedback from last year," said Capt. Timothy Kinsella, NAS Pensacola commanding officer, at a press briefing Tuesday. "Eight-hour waits to enter. Families giving up. That's not the experience we want to provide."

The solution: limit it. The base will allow a maximum of 25,000 vehicles across the two-day event, with entry controlled through a new online credentialing system. All adults 18 and older must pre-register through the Defense Biometric Identification System portal by April 20. Those without credentials will be turned away at the gate.

The security math

Base officials say the changes stem from updated Department of Defense force protection requirements implemented in January, not crowd control preferences. The 2019 shooting at NAS Pensacola that killed three sailors and wounded eight others led to years of incremental security tightening. This is the latest step.

"Every person entering this installation will be vetted against federal databases," Kinsella said. "That takes time. We've calculated our processing capacity, and 25,000 vehicles is our ceiling."

At an estimated 2.8 people per vehicle, that translates to roughly 70,000 on-base spectators across the weekend. Previous homecoming shows have drawn between 150,000 and 200,000 total attendees, according to Pensacola tourism officials.

The math leaves a gap. Someone has to go somewhere else.

The beach alternative

Escambia County and Santa Rosa Island Authority officials have been preparing since January, when the Navy first shared its revised security protocols.

Pensacola Beach will serve as the primary overflow viewing location. The Blue Angels' demonstration box extends over Santa Rosa Sound, making Casino Beach and Quietwater Beach viable spectator spots with unobstructed sightlines to roughly 60% of the show routine.

"You won't see the ground displays. You won't see the static aircraft," said Paolo Ghio, Santa Rosa Island Authority executive director. "But you'll see the diamond formation. You'll see the sneak pass. For a lot of families, that's enough."

The county is adding 400 temporary parking spaces in vacant lots along Via de Luna Drive and running free shuttle loops from Quietwater to Casino Beach every 15 minutes during show hours. Both beach pavilions will carry the show's radio broadcast on speakers.

Pensacola Beach businesses are already adjusting. Flounder's has added 30 seats to its deck. The Sandshaker is promoting a "Blue Angels Brunch" package both days. Several vacation rental managers report April bookings up 40% over 2025.

What the changes mean

For longtime airshow attendees, the new system represents a break from decades of tradition. The homecoming show has operated as a walk-up event since its inception in 1946, with entry limited only by traffic capacity and patience.

Military families with base access will still enter through normal credentialing. The pre-registration requirement applies to general public visitors using the temporary airshow gates on Navy Boulevard and Blue Angel Parkway.

The registration process requires a valid driver's license or state ID, vehicle registration and proof of insurance. Background checks typically clear within 48 hours, according to base officials, though those with common names or previous military service records may face delays requiring manual review.

Children under 18 can enter under a registered adult's credentials without individual screening.

"We understand this is an adjustment," Kinsella said. "We're asking people to plan ahead. But once you're registered, entry will be faster than it's ever been."

The base is projecting 45-minute maximum gate waits for pre-registered visitors, compared to the three to four hours common in recent years.

The downtown option

Downtown Pensacola offers another viewing alternative, though a more limited one. The team's high-altitude maneuvers are visible from Palafox Pier and Veterans Memorial Park, but most low-level passes occur too far south to track clearly from the urban core.

Several downtown restaurants are marketing rooftop viewing events regardless. The Pensacola Bay Center parking garage will open its upper decks free to the public during Saturday's show, per a city announcement Monday.

For those determined to see the full demonstration, on-base access remains the only option. Registration opens April 10 at the NAS Pensacola website. Base officials expect slots to fill within days.

The Blue Angels will fly their full demonstration both days, weather permitting, with shows scheduled for 2 p.m. Gates open at 8 a.m. The National Naval Aviation Museum will operate normal hours but with airshow-day access restrictions.

It's a homecoming. Just a more complicated one.

articles.label: Education
articles.headline: Escambia schools superintendent makes final pitch for $180 million bond before May referendum
articles.dek: With aging HVAC systems and overcrowded classrooms in the Beulah corridor, district leaders say the half-cent sales tax is overdue. Opponents call the timing tone-deaf.
brief: Escambia County School District officials are making their final push for a half-cent sales tax referendum that would generate $180 million over 10 years for facility improvements.
Superintendent Keith Leonard cited 47 schools with HVAC systems past their expected lifespan and projected enrollment growth of 2,400 students in the Beulah corridor by 2032.
A coalition of local business owners and retirees opposes the measure, arguing the timing ignores economic pressures on working families.
articles.body:
The air conditioning unit at Warrington Elementary School is older than most of the teachers who work there. Installed in 1991, it wheezes through Gulf Coast summers, breaking down three times last year alone. Maintenance crews patch what they can. The district keeps it on the replacement list.

That list is long.

On Tuesday night at the Pensacola State College campus in Warrington, Escambia County Schools Superintendent Keith Leonard stood before roughly 200 residents and laid out his case for a half-cent sales tax increase that would appear on the May 19 ballot. The revenue—projected at $180 million over a decade—would fund new construction, major renovations and the replacement of aging mechanical systems across the district's 68 schools.

"We are not asking for luxuries," Leonard said, gesturing toward a slideshow of cracked portable classrooms and rusted ductwork. "We are asking for functioning buildings."

The scope of the problem

According to district documents provided to The Flightline, 47 schools have HVAC systems that have exceeded their 25-year expected lifespan. Fourteen schools rely on portables for regular classroom instruction. The average age of district facilities is 42 years.

The most acute pressure is in the Beulah corridor, where residential development has outpaced school capacity. Beulah Elementary operates at 118 percent capacity. Beulah Middle sits at 109 percent. District projections estimate an additional 2,400 students will enroll in the corridor by 2032 if current growth trends continue.

Leonard told attendees the bond would fund construction of a new K-8 school in Beulah, slated to open by fall 2030. It would also pay for comprehensive HVAC replacements at 23 schools, roof repairs at 31 facilities and electrical upgrades to support modern classroom technology.

"This isn't about one neighborhood," Leonard said. "Every corner of this county has buildings that need attention."

Opponents organize

Not everyone in the room was convinced.

A coalition calling itself Escambia Families First has mounted an organized opposition to the referendum. The group, which includes local business owners and retirees, argues the sales tax increase would disproportionately burden working families already struggling with inflation and rising property insurance costs.

"People are choosing between groceries and gas," said Linda Hargrove, a retired teacher from Cantonment who spoke during the public comment period. "This is the wrong time to ask them for more."

Hargrove and other opponents have questioned whether the district has adequately explored alternative funding sources, including state grants and impact fees on new residential development. They point to a 2024 state report showing Escambia County collects among the lowest school impact fees in Florida.

School Board member Paul Shortridge, who represents District 3 and has expressed reservations about the referendum's timing, acknowledged those concerns but stopped short of opposing the measure outright.

"The needs are real," Shortridge said after the meeting. "The question is whether this is the right mechanism at the right moment."

What the tax would mean

The half-cent sales tax would apply to most retail purchases in Escambia County, excluding groceries and medicine. The district estimates the average household would pay approximately $120 more per year.

If approved, the tax would take effect January 1, 2027 and expire after 10 years unless renewed by voters.

Similar referendums have passed in neighboring Santa Rosa County and in Bay County to the east. Both districts have used the revenue to fund new school construction in high-growth areas.

Leonard noted that Escambia remains one of the few large Florida districts without a dedicated local sales tax for school facilities.

"We've been making do," he said. "Making do has a cost."

Final weeks of campaigning

With 40 days until the referendum, both sides are intensifying their outreach. The district has scheduled six more community forums, including sessions in Pensacola Beach, Molino and Brent. Escambia Families First has launched a social media campaign and begun distributing flyers at local grocery stores.

Early voting begins May 9. Registered voters can cast ballots at any early voting location in Escambia County through May 16.

Back at Warrington Elementary, principal Denise Rawlings said she tries not to think about the politics. She thinks about September, when temperatures in her building regularly hit 85 degrees before noon.

"The kids don't care about referendums," Rawlings said. "They just want to learn without sweating through their shirts."
